name: Atlantic Puffin
fact: Puffins can hold up to 62 sand eels crosswise in their bill at once, thanks to a specially hinged jaw and backward-pointing tongue spines.

name: Razorbill
fact: Razorbills are monogamous and often pair for life, returning to the same cliff ledge each breeding year.

name: Common Guillemot
fact: Guillemot eggs are pyriform (pear-shaped) so they spin in an arc rather than rolling off cliff ledges.

name: Black Guillemot
fact: Unlike most auks, Black Guillemots rarely venture far from shore and nest in coastal rock crevices and stone walls.

name: Little Auk
fact: Little Auks breed in the high Arctic in colonies of millions, but winter at sea off British coasts, easily missed in rough weather.

name: Northern Gannet
fact: Gannets plunge-dive at over 100 km/h and have no external nostrils; impact is absorbed by subcutaneous air sacs.

name: European Shag
fact: Shags have no waterproofing oil and must spread their wings to dry after diving — a posture known as 'wing-spreading'.

name: Great Cormorant
fact: Great Cormorants were trained by fishermen in China and Japan over 1,400 years ago — the practice still continues ceremonially today.

name: Manx Shearwater
fact: Manx Shearwaters navigate back to their home burrow from 5,000 km with near-perfect accuracy and can live over 50 years.

name: Sooty Shearwater
fact: Sooty Shearwaters undertake one of the longest migrations of any bird — a figure-of-eight circuit of the entire Pacific Ocean.

name: Balearic Shearwater
fact: The Balearic Shearwater is Critically Endangered — the entire world population breeds only in the Balearic Islands.

name: European Storm Petrel
fact: Storm Petrels 'walk' on the water surface while feeding, leading sailors to name them after St Peter.

name: Leach's Storm Petrel
fact: Leach's Storm Petrels have a deeply forked tail and an erratic, bounding flight quite unlike any other British seabird.

name: Northern Fulmar
fact: Fulmars defend their nest by projectile-vomiting a foul-smelling stomach oil up to a metre at intruders.

name: Great Skua
fact: Great Skuas are aggressive kleptoparasites that will repeatedly strike humans who approach their nest — including low-flying aircraft.

name: Arctic Skua
fact: Arctic Skuas come in pale and dark morphs; chasing Terns and forcing them to drop fish mid-air is their primary feeding strategy.

name: Pomarine Skua
fact: Adult Pomarine Skuas have distinctive spoon-shaped central tail feathers — the feature gives the species its common name.

name: Black-legged Kittiwake
fact: The Kittiwake is named for its call. Unlike most gulls it is almost entirely pelagic outside the breeding season.

name: European Herring Gull
fact: Herring Gull chicks instinctively peck at the red spot on the parent's bill to trigger regurgitation — a reflex proven experimentally by Tinbergen.

name: Great Black-backed Gull
fact: The world's largest gull. Great Black-backed Gulls regularly predate seabirds up to the size of a Puffin, swallowing them whole.

name: Lesser Black-backed Gull
fact: Lesser Black-backed Gulls were once entirely migratory but British birds increasingly overwinter, a shift tracked since the 1960s.

name: Common Gull
fact: Despite its name, the Common Gull is not the most abundant British gull — Herring and Black-headed Gulls both outnumber it.

name: Black-headed Gull
fact: The Black-headed Gull's 'black' hood is actually dark chocolate-brown, and it is absent entirely in winter plumage.

name: Mediterranean Gull
fact: The Mediterranean Gull has a pure white wingtip with no black — distinguishing it from the Black-headed Gull at a glance.

name: Little Gull
fact: The Little Gull is the world's smallest gull. Adults have a distinctive black underwing that flashes conspicuously in flight.

name: Arctic Tern
fact: Arctic Terns migrate ~90,000 km per year pole-to-pole — over a lifetime this exceeds three trips to the Moon and back.

name: Common Tern
fact: Common Terns can live over 30 years and return to the same colony annually with remarkable nest-site fidelity.

name: Sandwich Tern
fact: Sandwich Terns are named after Sandwich in Kent, where the species was first scientifically described in 1784.

name: Roseate Tern
fact: The Roseate Tern is one of Britain's rarest breeding seabirds; the UK population is largely confined to a handful of Irish Sea islands.

name: Little Tern
fact: Little Terns nest on open shingle beaches — one of Britain's most exposed and predator-vulnerable nesting sites.

name: Red-throated Diver
fact: Red-throated Divers are the only diver able to take off from land — all other species require a long water run-up.

name: Great Northern Diver
fact: The haunting wail of the Great Northern Diver is the defining sound of North American wilderness and Scottish sea lochs in winter.
